title: Polymer clay faux turquoise cabochon
subtitle: A veined turquoise-effect stone substitute in polymer clay
excerpt: A cabochon made from polymer clay textured and coloured to resemble natural turquoise, with a characteristic dark matrix vein pattern. The veining is produced by crumbling dark clay into the base colour before conditioning. The finished cabochon is approximately 25 mm diameter and can be used as a pendant, set in a bezel, or glued to a ring blank.
sourceNotes: Faux turquoise polymer clay technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that cures hard in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.
A smoothly polished gemstone or imitation stone shape with a flat back and a domed top, set in jewellery without faceting.

content.text: Natural turquoise has a distinctive blue-green body colour cut through by dark veining from the host rock matrix. In

, this effect is produced by working crumbled dark clay into a partially conditioned blue-green base. The dark crumbles distribute unevenly through the base, forming irregular veins. The result is pressed into a dome-topped
shape, cured, and polished. No kiln required.

items.name: Polymer clay, black or dark brown

content.text: Condition the blue-green clay until pliable but not fully smooth: leave it slightly crumbly and rough at the surface. This incomplete conditioning state is intentional. Crumble the 3 g of black clay into small rough pieces about 2-3 mm across and scatter them over the surface of the blue-green clay.

Fold the blue-green clay once over the scattered dark crumbles, pressing them into the surface. Roll the clay into a log and fold it once more. Do not over-condition: the goal is to keep the dark pieces elongated and irregular, running through the blue-green in vein-like streaks. Two folds distribute the veining sufficiently. More than four folds will blend the dark pieces in too thoroughly and the matrix pattern will disappear.

Roll the veined clay into a ball approximately 25 mm in diameter. Press it flat on one side on the wooden board to create the flat back of the cabochon. The domed top is left as the rounded upper surface of the ball. The finished cabochon should be about 25 mm diameter and 12 mm tall.
Place the cabochon flat-side down on a dedicated oven liner. Bake at 110-130°C for 25-30 minutes. Allow to cool fully before handling.

Sand the domed surface with 400-grit sandpaper under running water to avoid dust. The wet sanding reveals the veining pattern clearly. Move to 800-grit for the final polish, then buff with a soft cloth until the surface has a smooth stone-like sheen. The sanding is what makes the piece look like polished turquoise rather than painted clay.
Make a ring by gluing the cabochon to a ring blank with strong adhesive.
Thread a cord through a pierced hole in the cabochon for a pendant necklace.
Use a terracotta red base with black veining for a faux carnelian or red jasper effect.

rows.problem: Dark veins blend in and disappear
rows.cause: Too many folds during the veining step, over-blending the dark clay
rows.fix: Limit to two folds maximum when distributing the dark crumbles. The partial conditioning prevents full blending.

rows.cause: Sandpaper grit sequence skipped from coarse to fine without intermediate steps
rows.fix: Sand through 400-grit, then 800-grit, in order. Skipping steps leaves micro-scratches that prevent a clear sheen.

rows.cause: The ball was not pressed straight down when forming the flat back
rows.fix: Press on a flat board with an even downward pressure. Use a flat piece of card on top of the cabochon and press with the palm.
